coredata.dc:title: Development of high-dispersion CLDH/carbon dot composites to boost chloride binding of cement
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: He, Chuang
Sun, Tianying
Wang, Xiaogang
He, Haijie
E, Shuang
coredata.dc:description: Chloride-induced corrosion of steel reinforcement significantly contributes to the durability deterioration for reinforced concrete (RC) structures. Calcined layered double hydroxide (CLDH) possesses a distinctive structure memory effect to adsorb anions and a nucleation effect to facilitate cement hydration, thereby demonstrating a promising potential for chloride binding enhancement of cementitious materials. However, CLDH suffers from severe agglomeration in cement composites, limiting its performance efficacy. To tackle this problem, novel high-dispersity CLDH/carbon dots (CDs) composites (CC) only at small dosages are applied to greatly improve the chloride binding behavior of cement pastes for the first time. In detail, the specific surface area of CC synthesized by simply stirring CDs and CLDH improves by 166.4 % than that of pure CLDH. Remarkably, cement pastes containing CC display a preferable chloride binding capacity; And the chloride binding substantially increases by 52.41 % when the incorporation of CC (by weight of cement) is 0.8 wt%. More importantly, according to the phase composite and microstructure analyses, the chloride binding enhancement mechanism of CC-containing cement pastes is reasonably ascribed to two effects of CC: providing nucleation sites to accelerate cement hydration and exerting the inherent chloride adsorption capacity. This boosts the formation of C–S–H gels and improves the structure memory effect and anions exchange-induced chloride adsorption, thus significantly strengthening the chloride binding of cement. This work provides a novel approach to improve the dispersity of CLDH, effectively strengthening the chloride binding of cement, thereby conductive to prolonging the service life of RC structures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Calcined layered double hydroxides (CLDH)
Carbon dots (CDs)
Dispersity
Chloride binding
Cement

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Syntheses of CLDH, CDs, and CC
Characterization of CDs, CLDH, and CC

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China-Youth Science Fund (No. 52208273). the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (No. LQ21E080003), and \u201CPioneer\u201D and \u201CLeading Goose\u201D R&D Program of Zhejiang (No. 2022C03051; 2023C04033). Furthermore, the authors thank Xinfang Cui from Shiyanjia Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for FTIR analyses.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and topography of CDs: (a) TEM image (with a size distribution of CDs, inset); (b) HRTEM image; (c) AFM image; (d) Height profile along the white line in AFM image.
(a) FTIR spectra of CDs, CC, and CLDH; (b) DTG and TGA curves of CLDH and CC.
Typical TEM images of (a) CLDH and (c) CC; HRTEM images of (b) CLDH and (d) CC.
(a) AFM image of CLDH; (b) the height profile along the white line in the (a) image; (c) AFM image of CC; (d) the height profile along the white line in the (c) image.
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of CLDH and CC.
The C b values of cement pastes after (a) 9-day and (b) 14-day immersion.
XRD patterns of various samples (a) before immersion and (b) after 14-day immersion in 3 mol/L NaCl solution (CH: Ca(OH)2, Cc: CaCO3, Fs: Friedel's salt).
(a) TGA and (b) DTG curves of different cement pastes after 14-day immersion in NaCl solution.
Chloride distribution of Fs and C–S–H gels/CLDH.
SEM images of diverse pastes curing 28 d: (a) C0; (b) C2; (c) C3; (d) CC2; (e) CC3.
Schematic of the CLDH and CC improving chloride binding mechanism.

head.ce:title: Development of high-dispersion CLDH/carbon dot composites to boost chloride binding of cement

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Civil Engineering and Architecture, Taizhou University, Taizhou, Zhejiang, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Civil Engineering and Architecture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Civil Engineering and Architecture, Taizhou University, Taizhou, Zhejiang, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science, Dalian Minzu University, Dalian, Liaoning, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Science, Dalian Minzu University, Dalian, Liaoning, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chloride-induced corrosion of steel reinforcement significantly contributes to the durability deterioration for reinforced concrete (RC) structures. Calcined layered double hydroxide (CLDH) possesses a distinctive structure memory effect to adsorb anions and a nucleation effect to facilitate cement hydration, thereby demonstrating a promising potential for chloride binding enhancement of cementitious materials. However, CLDH suffers from severe agglomeration in cement composites, limiting its performance efficacy. To tackle this problem, novel high-dispersity CLDH/carbon dots (CDs) composites (CC) only at small dosages are applied to greatly improve the chloride binding behavior of cement pastes for the first time. In detail, the specific surface area of CC synthesized by simply stirring CDs and CLDH improves by 166.4 % than that of pure CLDH. Remarkably, cement pastes containing CC display a preferable chloride binding capacity; And the chloride binding substantially increases by 52.41 % when the incorporation of CC (by weight of cement) is 0.8 wt%. More importantly, according to the phase composite and microstructure analyses, the chloride binding enhancement mechanism of CC-containing cement pastes is reasonably ascribed to two effects of CC: providing nucleation sites to accelerate cement hydration and exerting the inherent chloride adsorption capacity. This boosts the formation of C–S–H gels and improves the structure memory effect and anions exchange-induced chloride adsorption, thus significantly strengthening the chloride binding of cement. This work provides a novel approach to improve the dispersity of CLDH, effectively strengthening the chloride binding of cement, thereby conductive to prolonging the service life of RC structures.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Reinforced concrete (RC) structures are extensively employed worldwide benefiting from their advantages such as cost-effectiveness, superior strength, commendable structural integrity and ease of construction [1–3]. However, they still face critical durability challenges [4,5]. This significantly reduces their lifespan [6,7], giving rise to significant security concerns and enormous economic losses. It is commonly accepted that the principal reason causing the durability degradation of RC structures is the chloride-induced corrosion of reinforcement, particularly in coastal and deicing salts-applied environments [8]. In detail, when the chloride concentration on the reinforcement surface exceeds the threshold, the protective layer of reinforcement tends to be damaged, resulting in reinforcement corrosion, thereby inducing cracks in RC structures [8]. To surmount the barrier above, diverse corrosion prevention strategies have been developed, such as functional coatings [9], corrosion inhibitors [10], and cathodic protection [11], to mitigate chloride-caused corrosion. Regrettably, these techniques fail to effectively eradicate the intrusive chloride ions within cement composites. This brings about repeated destruction of the passivation layer on the reinforcement steel surface induced by the intruded chloride ions. Hence, these strategies above inadequately prevent chloride-caused corrosion. It is imperative to explore a cost-effective and efficacious approach to consume the penetrated chloride ions within cement matrices, thus fundamentally inhibiting chloride-induced corrosion.

Exhilaratingly, chloride binding is recognized as one of the most cost-effective ways to transform intruded free chloride ions into immobilized chloride ions [12,13]. More specifically, this approach efficiently binds free chloride ions through two mechanisms: physical adsorption primarily caused by C–S–H gels; and chemical binding mainly induced by Friedel's salt (Fs) (generated through interaction between SiO4-AFm and free chloride ions) [12]. Up to now, nanomaterials have been utilized to markedly strengthen the chloride binding ability of cement composites, benefiting by their high specific surface area and strong reactivity, including nano-SiO2 [14,15], nano-Al2O3 [12,16], carbon nanotubes [17,18], and layered double hydroxide (LDH) [19–21]. Among them, LDH has attracted more attention due to its interlayer anion exchange properties, structural tunability, thermal stability, and nucleation effect [19–22]. While calcined LDH (CLDH) fabricated by calcinating LDH not only can provide more active sites for cement hydration but also displays a distinctive structural memory characteristic, allowing it to adsorb chloride ions [23,24]. As a result, CLDH is expected to further strengthen the chloride binding property of cement compared with LDH [21]. For example, Jin's group evidenced that adding 7.5 wt% CLDH into seawater sand mortar effectively elevated the chloride binding action by 65 % [25]. Subsequently, Li's group indicated that the incorporation of 6.0 wt% CLDH enhanced the chloride binding of cement after 28-day curing by 43.1 % [26]. Nonetheless, CLDH is susceptible to poor dispersity in cement matrices, thereby restricting their positive effect on chloride binding. More seriously, to attain the desirable enhancement effect, compensatory addition of CLDH to cement matrices is necessary, resulting in a higher dosage, thus substantially escalating the expense. To elevate the dispersity of CLDH, some dispersion treatments including surface modification [27] and ultrasonic treatment [28] have been developed. For instance, CLDH modified with tetraethylenepentamine presented a significantly improved dispersity, thereby demonstrating a higher adsorption ability [27]. Furthermore, the highly dispersed CLDH obtained by ultrasonic processing exhibited higher anion adsorption capacity [28]. However, the aforementioned approaches fail to be regarded as ideal dispersion methods due to their inherent limitations, such as severe reaction conditions, complex operational steps, and significant time and energy demands. Hence, it is urgently required to develop a facile, low-cost, and effective method to prepare high-dispersion CLDH, and investigate its promotion effect on chloride binding of cementitious materials.

Fortunately, the combination of carbon dots (CDs) and LDH to generate a novel composite contributes to increasing the dispersity of LDH. More importantly, CDs as a new zero-dimensional nanomaterial possess multitudes of merits such as low cost, favorable dispersity, low toxicity, and environmentally friendly [29–34]. A great number of references have evidenced that CDs/LDH composites hold a considerable prospect in enhancing the adsorption efficacy of anionic species [35–37]. Specifically, Zhang's group directly assembled CDs with abundant oxygen-containing groups onto the LDH surface to synthesize CDs/LDH composites for greatly absorbing methyl blue [35]. Ge's group successfully prepared CDs@MgAl-LDH by coating CDs onto the surface of LDH, and unveiled that CDs@MgAl-LDH with a good dispersity in water could effectively remove various dyes [37]. Moreover, Rahamanian's group revealed that graphene quantum dots (GQDs, a type of CDs)/LDH composites exhibited remarkable efficacy in adsorbing heavy metal ions [36]. Given that CLDH possesses the structural memory effect, it is reasonable to hypothesize that CDs/CLDH composites (CC) possibly own comparable or even stronger anion adsorption efficacy than CDs/LDH composites, thereby beneficial to greatly strengthening the chloride binding ability of cement system. Recently, He's group employed GQDs-assisted ultrasonication to exfoliate CLDH to improve chloride binding performance. Although the as-obtained CLDH with high dispersity enhance the chloride binding by more than 50 %, the ultrasonic method assisted by GQDs had inevitable disadvantages such as preparation complexity of GQDs and special ultrasound equipment [38]. Therefore, it is highly urgently to develop a straightforward and effective approach to prepare high-dispersity CC and subsequently investigate their influence on the chloride binding of cement.

In light of this, this study successfully prepares high-dispersity CC by only simply stirring CDs and CLDH, and firstly testifies their improvement on chloride binding of cement pastes. More importantly, the improvement mechanism of CC-containing samples is reasonably proposed. Specifically, the dispersity performance of CC is determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), and N2 adsorption-desorption (NAD) measurement. While the chloride binding ability of diverse cement pastes is investigated by the equilibrium method. Based on the analysis of X-ray diffractometer (XRD), thermogravimetric analysis/differential thermogravimetric (TGA/DTG), and scanning electron microscope (SEM), the mechanism of CC-caused chloride binding mechanism is rationally revealed. This study demonstrates a considerable potential of CC for enhancing chloride binding of cement pastes, thus contributing to extending the service life of RC structures.

Portland cement (PC) with a class of P.I 52.5R was purchased by Wuhan SinoCem Smartec Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, Hubei Province, China). The physical properties and chemical compositions were provided in Tables 1 and 2
, respectively. Citric acid (CA), urea and Mg/Al–CO3 LDH (Mg6Al2(OH)16CO3·4H2O) were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China), and used directly without further purification. During all the experiments, deionized (DI) water with a resistance of 18 MΩ cm−1 was employed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Syntheses of CLDH, CDs, and CC
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the methodology described in the literature [39] LDH was subject to calcination in a muffle furnace at a temperature of 500 °C for 3 h, formatting CLDH powder upon subsequent cooling to ambient temperature.

CDs were synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal approach [40]. In detail, a mixture comprising 4 g of CA and urea in a 3:1 proportion was introduced into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave with a volume of 150 mL. Then the mixture underwent heating at a temperature of 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture obtained from the autoclave was centrifuged and then passed through a 0.2 μm membrane filter to remove insoluble products, large particles, and unreacted small molecules. Ultimately, the dark brown powder CDs were prepared after concentration by rotary evaporation and freeze-dried under a vacuum.

CC were obtained by the mechanical stirring of CLDH and CDs in a 4:1 ratio. In detail, 12 g of CLDH and 3 g of CDs powders were added to 400 mL deionized water. And subsequently the as-obtained mixture underwent ultrasonic treatment to facilitate the rapid dispersion of the aggregated CLDH. Following this, the mixture was dispersed using a magnetic stirrer for 1 h to accelerate effective combine between CDs and CLDH. The resultant solution was then filtered through a 0.45 μm filter membrane to eliminate the surplus CDs dissolved in the water. The filtered CC solids were subsequently subject to freeze-drying to obtain the dried CC powders for subsequent experimental investigations. The preparation process of CC composites is visually illustrated in Fig. 1 .

To conduct a thorough examination of the impact of CLDH and CC on the chloride binding performance of cement composites, seven samples with varous dosages were prepared. CLDH and CC were incorporated into the cement in different proportions (0, 0.1 wt%, 0.3 wt%, and 0.8 wt%, based on the weight of cement). Accordingly, the resulting mixtures were labeled as C0, C1, C2, C3, CC1, CC2, and CC3. The specific mixture proportions were presented in Table 3 . The specific preparation procedure was as follows: Initially, the cement was subject to mechanical stirring within a container for 30 s. Following this, DI, CLDH solution, and CC solution were introduced into the mixing apparatus under a water-cement ratio (W/C) of 0.5. The mixing procedure involved low-speed blending for 120 s, followed by suspension for 15 s, and concluded with high-speed blending for another 120 s. It was important to mention that no additional admixtures were introduced during this process. Subsequently, the mixed pastes were poured into a mold measuring 20 mm × 20 mm × 20 mm. These specimens were placed in a standard curing chamber maintained at 20 ± 2 °C with a relative humidity exceeding 95 %. After 24 h, the specimens were removed from the molds and continued to cure for 28 d.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs, CLDH, and CC
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of CDs, CLDH, and CC were analyzed using TEM (FEI Talos F200S) and AFM (Brucker MTP-3D infinity). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet-6700, USA) and XRD (Brucker D8 Advance, Germany) were utilized to elucidate the structural information of CDs, CLDH, and CC. N2 adsorption-desorption (NAD) measurement was taken on the NOVA4200E micropore instrument (Quantchrorome, USA), and the specific surface area of CLDH and CC was calculated in light of the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) theory. Furthermore, the zeta potential test was performed to detect the surface charges and interaction between CDs and CLDH. Additionally, the chemical composition and thermal stability of CLDH and CC was investigated through the TGA/DTG.

The chloride binding capacity of cement pastes was tested by the chloride adsorption equilibrium method [41]. Specifically, the cement pastes underwent a curing period of 28 d, subsequently were vacuum-dried at 60 °C until their mass stabilized. Afterward, the dried samples were ground into a uniform powder with particle sizes below 75 μm to allow cement powder fully contact with NaCl solution. 4 g of the cement powder was then added into a centrifuge tube containing 40 mL 3 mol/L NaCl solution. Then, the mixture was agitated to ensure uniform blending. After agitation, the tubes were sealed and positioned in an gloomy environment with a temperature range of 20 ± 2 °C. To guarantee sufficient interaction, the centrifuge tubes were manually agitated every 12 h for 2 min. Subsequently, after immersion of 9 and 14 d, the mixture underwent centrifugation at a speed of 5000 revolutions per minute for 5 min to separate the supernatant liquid. Based on the Chinese standard JGJT 322–2013, 1 mL of the supernatant solution was extracted for determining the concentration of residual chloride ions. This was accomplished using automatic potentiometric titration with a titrant composed of 0.1 M silver nitrate (AgNO3). To guarantee the accuracy and dependability of the experimental findings, each sample underwent ten titrations. The calculation of the associated bound chloride ion content (C b) was performed utilizing Equation (1) .
(1)
C b
=
(
C 0
‐
C 1
)
×
V 0
× 35.45
M

The variables C b, denoted the bound chloride content (mg/g paste), C 0 and C 1 represented the initial and the final chloride ions concentration of the NaCl solutions (M). V 0 and M represented the volume of the NaCl solution (mL) and the mass of the cement powder (g), respectively.

XRD, TGA/DTG and SEM were employed to examine the alterations in the phase composition of various samples before and following immersion, revealing the potential of CLDH and CC at different dosages to enhance chloride binding capacity. The samples were prepared by drying the centrifuged sediment in a vacuum oven at 60 °C until a constant mass was achieved. Afterward, it was subjected to filtration using a 75 μm sieve. The XRD patterns of the samples before and after immersion were analyzed using a Brucker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer from Germany. The analysis was conducted under the Cu Kα radiation source, with a scanning speed of 6°/min. TGA/DTG curves were obtained by heating the specimens from 30 to 1000 °C with a rate of 10 °C/min in N2 atmosphere, utilizing an STA-409PC TGA. The microstructure of the specimens was examined using a MIRA LMS SEM, (TESCAN) before and after immersion. Additionally, all samples were coated with gold before testing to enhance the quality of the SEM images.

The morphology and topography of as-prepared CDs are investigated through TEM and AFM images. The TEM image (Fig. 2 a) reveals that CDs are spherical and uniformly distributed, with an average particle size of approximately 2.0 nm and sizes varying between 1.3 and 3.7 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 2b) displays that CDs possess an apparent lattice fringe with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, consistent with the (100) plane of graphite. As displayed in AFM image (Fig. 2c), it further substantiates that the as-obtained CDs possess outstanding dispersity. And CDs present a topographical height between 0.80 and 2.95 nm (Fig. 2d), indicating that CDs possess a dual or few-layer graphite structure, and are approximately spherical.

The surface groups of CDs are assessed by FTIR spectra. As depicted in Fig. 3 , the broad peak at 3200–3400 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H [42]. And the peaks at 1660, 1553, 1400, and 1065 cm−1 verify the presence of CO, N–H, C–N, and C–O respectively [43–45]. Furthermore, the peaks at 2982 and 2863 cm−1 are attributed to stretching vibration of C–H [44]. FTIR analysis confirms the presence of numerous N- and O-containing groups on the surface of CDs, endowing CDs with superior water solubility.

To sum up, the CDs synthesized by the one-pot hydrothermal method possess a small size of ca 2.0 nm, a favorable monodispersity, and abundant surface groups. These characteristics conduce to augmenting their adsorption on CLDH surface and efficiently combining with CLDH.

It is widely accepted that the Zeta potential between nanoparticles is crucial for understanding their interaction [46]. Therefore, the Zeta potentials of CLDH, CDs, and CC in aqueous solution are measured. In detail, the surface charge of CLDH is positive, with a Zeta potential of +33.7 mV. While the surface charge of CDs is negative, displaying a Zeta potential of −18.9 mV. Notably, the Zeta potential measured in CC is +22.8 mV, indicating the successful combination of CDs and CLDH through by electrostatic attraction.

FTIR is applied to reveal the surface groups of CLDH and CC (Fig. 4 a). In sharp contrast to CDs, both CLDH and CC exhibit a broad absorption peak around 3450 cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibrations of interlayer water and layered hydroxyl groups (−OH). Remarkably, CC possess identical peaks between 2800 and 2900 cm−1, originating from the stretching vibration of C–H in CDs, thereby providing evidence for the successful complexation of CDs with CLDH. Meanwhile, compared with CLDH, the peaks associated with CO and C–H stretching vibrations in CC exhibit a discernible displacement. This is probably ascribed to the electrostatic attraction between CDs and the CLDH surface, enhancing the dispersion of CLDH and subsequently increasing their interlayer spacing, thereby consequently affecting the vibration frequencies of their internal chemical bonds or functional groups [47].

The TGA/DTG curves of CLDH and CC are shown in Fig. 4b to investigate their chemical composition and thermal stability. Regarding DTG results, the mass loss of both CLDH and CC is evidently divided into two parts. For CLDH (going through the same stirring process as CC only without the addition of CDs), its mass loss peak occurred at around 215 °C due to the loss of interlayer water in their reconstructed structure, possessing 10.24 % of their total weight loss. The second mass loss peak occurs at around 375 °C, corresponding to the removal of layered hydroxyl (Mg/Al–OH) and the decomposition of their interlayer
CO 3
2 ‐
[47], leading to a mass loss of 20.19 %. In terms of CC, their first mass loss peak occurs at ca. 240 °C with a mass loss of about 17.81 %, dramatically higher than that of CLDH (10.24 %). This is mainly due to the removal of interlayer water molecules in CLDH and thermo decomposition of amino-groups of CDs. Additionally, an evident second mass loss peak is detected at approximately 430 °C, accounting for 30.28 % of their total weight loss, higher than that of CLDH (20.19 %). This is attributed to the decomposition of CDs adsorbed on the surface of CLDH in addition to the removal of layered hydroxyl and the decomposition of interlayer
CO 3
2 ‐
in CLDH. More importantly, the occurrence temperature of the mass loss peak in CC is significantly delayed compared with CLDH, suggesting that the electrostatic attraction between CDs and CLDH effectively augments their thermal stability. In brief, based on the FTIR and TGA/DTG analyses, CDs are successfully combined with CLDH.

TEM images (Fig. 5 ) provide a detailed understanding of CLDH and CC morphology. Specifically, CLDH tends to seriously aggregate and crosslink due to their large surface energy, thus forming elongated chains composed of multiple CLDH units (Fig. 5a). The length of CLDH aggregation is approximately 1800 nm, significantly larger than the average size of a single CLDH (less than 200 nm). According to the HRTEM image of CLDH (Fig. 5b), a great number of CLDHs stack together, resulting in the reduction of the dispersity and specific surface area. As shown in Fig. 5c, after the ultrasonic treatment between CLDH and CDs, CC display a remarkable decline in agglomeration and thickness, shortening their cross-linked units. This is expected to amplify the specific surface area of CC. In terms of the HRTEM image of CC (Fig. 5d), some small nanoparticles with a size of 2 nm belonging to CDs are observed on the surface of CC, confirming that CDs adhere to the surface of CLDH.

Additionally, the distribution and morphological heights of CLDH and CC are characterized by AFM images. Fig. 6 a shows that CLDH exhibits dense and severe cross-linking distribution, with morphological height ranging from 2.2 to 3.2 nm (Fig. 6b). This demonstrates the severe stacking and cross-linking of CLDH. Regarding CC shown in Fig. 6c, the lamellar-like CC is independently distributed, with a morphological height of 1.1–1.7 nm along the white line (Fig. 6d). Compared with CLDH, the morphological height of CC significantly reduces, suggesting that the aggregation and stacking phenomenon are effectively alleviated. Overall, both TEM and AFM test prove that combining CDs with CLDH can obviously enhance the dispersity of CLDH.

The specific surface area (S BET) of CLDH and CC are investigated using the NAD method and BET theory. The NAD isotherms are shown in Fig. 7 . Apparently, all the adsorption-desorption curves conform to type-IV adsorption characteristics with H3-type hysteresis loops, suggesting the presence of numerous mesoporous in CLDH and CC. Meanwhile, the adsorption-desorption curves of CC are significantly higher than that of CLDH, indicating that CC exhibit higher S BET than CLDH. More specifically, after further calculations, CC possess a superior S BET of 29.351 m2
∙
g−1, increasing by a dramatic 166.42 % than that of CLDH (11.017 m2
∙
g−1).

In conclusion, according to the aforementioned analyses of CLDH and CC characteristics, CDs are successfully combined with CLDH via electrostatic attraction. The combination of CDs and CLDH can effectively alleviate the aggregation, thus boosting the SBET and dispersity of CLDH. This provides a substantial basis for improving the chloride binding behavior of cement composites through CC-induced modification.

To evaluate the modification effect of CLDH and CC on the chloride binding capacity, the chloride binding content (C b) after immersing in 3 mol/L NaCl solution for 9 and 14 d are investigated via the typical equilibrium test. In Fig. 8 a, the addition of CLDH and CC apparently increases the C b values of modified pastes, and the more the dosage of CLDH or CC, the higher the C b values. More importantly, at the same dosage, the C b values of CC groups are consistently higher than those of CLDH groups. This indicates that both CLDH and CC significantly strengthen the ability of chloride binding in cement pastes, and CC exhibit a more obvious promoting effect. After 14-day immersion (Fig. 8b), the C b values of all specimens are higher than those at 9-day immersion. Furthermore, whether immersed for 9 or 14 d,the samples appear the identical variation trend. Specifically, under the highest dosage (0.8 wt%), the C b values of CC3 and C3 are 22.1 and 19.6 mg/g respectively, increasing by 52.41 % and 35.17 % than blank group (14.5 mg/g). Through the combination of CLDH and CDs, the modification efficiency of CLDH is enhanced by approximately 12.7 %. In summary, CC with preferable dispersity and higher specific surface area are more conducive to enhancing the ability of chloride binding in cement pastes than pure CLDH.

To clarify the enhancement mechanism of cement pastes containing CC, XRD, TGA/DTG, and SEM tests are executed to systematacially detecte the phase composition prior to and following a 14-day immersion. Since the same experimental method is utilized for each measurement, comparing the intensities of main characteristic peaks can qualitatively indicate the relative content [48]. As shown in Fig. 9 a, all the samples before immersion exhibit identical diffraction peaks, including Ca(OH)2 (CH, peaks at 18.1°, 28.7°, and 34°) and CaCO3 (Cc, peak at 29.4° unavoidably generated during the carbonation reaction between CH and CO2). This demonstrates that incorporating CLDH or CC fails to change the types of hydration products. Generally, the strength of the primary CH diffraction peak at 18.1° is proportional to its content [26]. Therefore, the stronger the 18.1° diffraction peak, the more the CH content and the higher the cement hydration degree. Relative to the C0 group, the CH diffraction peaks at 18.1° in CLDH- or CC-modified pastes exhibit higher intensity, indicating that both CLDH and CC are supposed to promote the cement hydration. Regardless of the incorporation of CLDH or CC, the intensity of CH peaks at 18.1° is enhanced as the dosage increases. It certifies that higher dosage possesses more effective modification effects within the range from 0 to 0.2 wt%. More importantly, at the same dosage, CC groups display stronger CH diffraction peaks than CLDH groups, and CC3 group present the highest intensity. This reflects that CC is more effective in advancing the hydration of cement than CLDH. Meanwhile, as one of the primary hydration products, the content of C–S–H gels is positively correlated with the degree of cement hydration [49]. As C–S–H gels are primarily responsible for physical adsorption of chloride ions via electrostatic or van der Waals forces [8], CC can more efficiently strength the physical adsorption of cement. After 14-day immersion (Fig. 9b), a new diffraction peaks of Fs (peaks at 11°, 22° and 23°) is observed. This indicates that the reaction between monosulfate (Ms; SiO4-AFm) and chloride ions, achieving chloride chemical binding, is accomplished [13]. Additionally, the intensity of CH peak at 18.1° is apparently reduced after 14-day immersion because the formation of Fs requires the participation of CH [50]. However, the peaks of Fs in all the samples show the similar intensity, suggesting that the incorporation of CLDH or CC slightly influence the chemical binding capacity of cement paste.

Since XRD test fails to detect the amorphous C–S–H gels, TGA/DTG is performed to gain more accurate information on phase composition of diverse pastes after 14-day immersion. As illustrated in the TGA curves (Fig. 10 a), the CLDH and CC groups exhibit larger mass loss compared with C0 group, and CC groups display more mass loss than CLDH groups. This demonstrated that the incorporation of CLDH and CC can augment the content of cement hydration products, and CC present the more significant modification effect. In addition, the weight loss peaks of 50–140 °C, 140–190 °C, 270–350 °C, 410–460 °C and 600–800 °C from all the samples are attributed to the disintegration of C–S–H/ettringite (AFt), Ms, Fs, CH and Cc, respectively [51,52]. It is widely recognized that the intensity of mass loss peaks is positively correlated with the content of the phases [31]. Based on the previous study that C–S–H gels are the predominant phase in cement paste after hydration [53], the mass loss of C–S–H/AFt peak can be considered as the mass loss of C–S–H gels. As displayed in Fig. 10b, CC3 group containing the highest dosage of CC presents the largest weight loss peak of C–S–H gels, followed by CC2, C3, C2 and C groups. This discloses that CC demonstrate greater efficiency than CLDH in facilitating cement hydration to generate C–S–H gels, thus endowing cement paste with more powerful physical adsorption capacity of chloride. In terms of Ms and Fs, there is no significant difference in the weight loss peaks of CLDH or CC groups, further unveiling that the introduction of CLDH or CC only slightly improves the chemical binding performance of cement. This is coincidence with the XRD results.

In general, the chloride binding in cementitious materials is categorized into two types: physical adsorption and chemical binding. Physical adsorption is achieved when free chloride ions are adsorbed by C–S–H gels, while chemical binding primarily involves the formation of Fs to immobilize free chloride ions. Apart from Fs and C–S–H gels, the chloride binding attributed to other phases is deemed insignificant [54]. To verify the chloride distribution of this two types, the chemical binding chloride (
C b Fs
) values is calculated by Equations (2) and (3) firstly. And then the physical adsorption chloride (
C b
CSH / CLDH
) is determined using Equation (4):
C b
CSH / CLDH
=
C b Total
−
C b Fs
. It should be noted that CLDH possess inherent chloride adsorption capacity by their structural reconstruction and anions exchange, and the chloride adsorption caused by CLDH is classified as physical adsorption. The chloride distribution after 14-day immersion is illustrated in Fig. 11 . Obviously, the incorporation of CLDH and CC can remarkably boost the values of
C b
CSH / CLDH
compared with C group. Meanwhile, the enhancing effect of CC is consistently superior to that of CLDH at the same dosage. In detail, CC3 present the highest
C b
CSH / CLDH
value (20.28 mg/g), followed by CC2 (18.61 mg/g), C3 (17.69 mg/g), C2 (15.97 mg/g) and C (12.58 mg/g). With respect to the chemical binding, the difference of
C b Fs
values between various samples is insignificant. This precisely corresponds to the minimal difference of Fs content observed in XRD and TG test. In brief, the forementioned phenomena evidence that the combination of CLDH and CDs can exceedingly promote the production of C–S–H gels, thereby intensely heightening the chloride binding capacity.

SEM test is applied to detect the microstructure of diverse cement pastes undergo 28-day curing (Fig. 12 ), presenting typical SEM images of various cement pastes before immersion. In terms of C0 group (Fig. 12a), the presence of a large number of unhydrated phases (C2S/C3S) with smooth surfaces indicates the inadequate hydration of cement [55]. In the CLDH-modified groups (Fig. 12b and c), needle-like AFt crystals as the typical hydration product can be observed. At the same time, the content of amorphous C–S–H gels is noticeably higher compared to C0 group, demonstrating that the addition of CLDH can effectively promote cement hydration. As for the specimen containing CC2 (Fig. 12d), the content of gelatinous and amorphous C–S–H gels pronouncedly increases. Meanwhile, unhydrated particles obviously decrease in size. As shown in Fig. 12e, the content of C–S–H gels further advances with the augmentation of dosage, and the unhydrated particles become almost invisible. These aforementioned results demonstrate from microscopic perspective that the addition of CDs and CLDH can effectively boost the production of C–S–H gels, thereby strengthening the physical adsorption performance of cement pastes.

In summary, the XRD, TGA/DTG, and SEM results substantiate that both the incorporation of CLDH and CC can promote the cement hydration, evidently strengthening the physical adsorption while tinily improving the chemical binding of cement paste. More importantly, the combination of CLDH and CDs confer larger specific surface area and better dispersity on CC. Therefore, CC possesse superior modification effects compared to CLDH.

With respect to the second pathway, CLDH can adsorb the chloride ions through their structural reconstruction and anions exchange [58,59]. However, CLDH easily suffer from the aggregation, tending to aggregate severely and crosslink. This results in an inadequate contact with chloride ions, thereby failing to maximize their chloride adsorption capacity. After the combination between the CLDH and CDs, the dispersity of CC is obviously improved, giving rising to the effective alleviation of the crosslink phenomenon. The AFM results show that the morphological height of CC (1.1−1.7 nm) obviously reduces compared with CLDH (2.2−3.2 nm). This allows CC to adequately contact with chloride ions, thus enhancing their inherent chloride adsorption capacity.

For the purpose of augmenting the resistance of RC structures against chloride-induced corrosion, high-dispersity CC are used to strengthen the capacity of chloride binding in cement composites for the first time. Furthermore, the enhancement mechanism is rationally put forward. The major conclusions are as follows.
(1)
CC are successfully prepared via simple mechanical stirring of CLDH and CDs. The combination of CLDH and CDs can alleviate the aggregation defect of CLDH, conferring high dispersity and high specific surface area on CC. The specific surface area of CC is 29.351 m2 g−1, increasing by 166.42 % compared with CLDH. These characteristics endow CC with the excellent prospect to strengthen the chloride binding ability of cement composites.
(2)
The C b of cement composites containing various dosages of CLDH and CC are determined via the typical equilibrium test. At the highest dosage (0.8 wt%), the C b values of CC and CLDH after 14-day immersion are 22.1 and 19.6 mg/g respectively, representing increases of 52.41 % and 35.17 % compared to the blank group (14.5 mg/g). The results indicate that the incorporation of both CC and CLDH can enhance the ability of chloride binding in cement composites, and the enhancement effect of CC is more efficient than that of pure CLDH.
(3)
Through the comprehensive material characterization and phases analyses, the CC-induced enhancement mechanism is reasonably attributed to two aspects. Firstly, CC with high specific surface area can provide extra nucleation sites to boost cement hydration, accelerating the formation of C–S–H gels. This conduces to improving the physical adsorption of cement paste. Secondly, CC with high dispersity can fully contact with chloride ions, strengthing the inherent chloride adsorption of CC.

This study signifies the feasibility of the CDs-assissted mechanical mixing method to disperse CLDH, and demonstrates that CC with high dispersity can significantly enhance the chloride binding capacity of cement composites. These observations are expected to provide a promising nanocomposite to strengthen the chloride corrosion resistance of RC structures. In addition, further investigations are required to explore the effects of the mass ratio of CDs to CLDH and the addition of CC on the chloride binding capacity, as well as the enhancement effect of CC on the mechanical property and chloride penetration resistance of cement pastes. Moreover, the mechanisms behind the CC-induced enhancement of chloride binding can be elucidated through molecular dynamics simulations.

Chuang He: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Tianying Sun: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Xiaogang Wang: Data curation. Haijie He: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Shuang E: Validation, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work. There is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China-Youth Science Fund (No. 52208273). the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (No. LQ21E080003), and “Pioneer” and “Leading Goose” R&D Program of Zhejiang (No. 2022C03051; 2023C04033). Furthermore, the authors thank Xinfang Cui from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for FTIR analyses.
